author: sproul
content:
<@755590043632140352> I fundamentally disagree that the spec shouldn’t handle “edge cases”. I could not care less about “readability” if it means there is ambiguity in cases that my code is meant to support. Gloas genesis is used in the spec tests themselves, so in a way we are already requiring a specific behaviour, it just isn’t defined anywhere other than the test generator (which IMO is not ideal)

I don’t understand your comment about the upgrade function either. That’s something that _will_ happen on mainnet and there _is_ a state root check at the fork??

Similarly for this issue: https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/issues/5096. I don’t see why we need to write the spec in a way that is obviously ambiguous/wrong  for the sake of readability

author: potuz
content: No, the state root is at the post state of the block. Not at the upgrade. The next state root check is after the first block is imported.

author: sproul
content: but that state root still depends on what happens in the upgrade function? if you did something different from the spec you could get a different post block state (yes if you did something convergent you would not, but how likely is that?)

author: sproul
content: also, if there’s a skipped slot, then that state root needs to be agreed (it goes into the state roots vector)
